behaviors.description: A cell containing =SUM(A1:A10) evaluates to the correct sum of values in cells A1 through A10
behaviors.justification: To catch real failures (incorrect range iteration, off-by-one errors, type coercion), the SUM function must resolve an actual range of cells within a cell grid. Unit testing SUM with mocked cell access could miss failures in how the formula parser interprets A1:A10 or how the range resolver accesses the cell grid. The real failure boundary is the interaction between formula parsing, range resolution, and cell access—multiple modules wired together.
behaviors.test_description: Create a spreadsheet with cells A1:A10 populated with known numeric values, create a cell containing the formula =SUM(A1:A10), evaluate it, and verify the result equals the expected sum. Test with edge cases: negative numbers, decimals, mixed types, and non-numeric cells (which should be ignored or error).

behaviors.description: A cell referencing another cell containing a formula produces the correct cascaded result (e.g., B1=A1*2, C1=B1+1)
behaviors.justification: The real failures are in dependency resolution and evaluation order: the engine must correctly identify that C1 depends on B1, B1 depends on A1, and evaluate in topological order. Mocking intermediate values would hide failures in how the dependency graph is built or traversed. Requires a cell grid with multiple interdependent formulas and a working evaluator.
behaviors.test_description: Create a chain of dependent formulas: A1=5, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+1. Evaluate C1 and verify it equals 11. Then change A1 to 10 and verify C1 automatically updates to 21. Test with longer chains and verify evaluation happens in correct order (not evaluating C1 before B1 is ready).

behaviors.description: The engine detects a circular dependency (A1→B1→A1) and returns an error instead of looping infinitely
behaviors.justification: The real failure is in cycle detection within the actual dependency graph. Unit testing a cycle-detection algorithm in isolation might pass, but fail when integrated with the formula evaluator if cycles aren't caught before evaluation begins. The failure boundary is the full dependency graph with actual cell references.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells where A1=B1+1 and B1=A1+1. Attempt to evaluate either cell and verify a circular reference error is raised (not a stack overflow or infinite loop). Test with longer cycles: A1→B1→C1→A1. Verify the error message identifies the cycle.

behaviors.description: VLOOKUP with exact match returns correct value; missing key returns error
behaviors.justification: Real failures include: wrong row returned (off-by-one), case sensitivity not handled, error not raised for missing keys, range interpreted incorrectly. VLOOKUP needs a real lookup table (range of cells) and actual formula evaluation context. Mocking a key-value store would miss failures in range parsing or boundary conditions.
behaviors.test_description: Create a lookup table (e.g., 3 rows × 2 columns with names and IDs). Create a cell with =VLOOKUP("Alice", A1:B3, 2, FALSE). Verify it returns the correct ID. Test with missing key and verify appropriate error. Test case sensitivity, partial matches (if applicable), and boundary conditions (first/last row of table).

behaviors.description: IF function correctly handles nested conditions (e.g., =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"))
behaviors.justification: While the IF logic itself could be unit-tested with direct boolean inputs, the real failure in context involves condition evaluation against cell values. Testing the full formula with cell references catches failures in how conditions are short-circuited, how cell values are coerced to booleans, and the evaluation order of nested IFs. This is more realistic to the stated behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Create cell A1 with value 15, then a cell B1 with =IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low"). Verify B1 evaluates to "medium". Test with A1=5 (expect "low") and A1=25 (expect "high"). Verify no short-circuit failures (both conditions are evaluated as needed).

behaviors.description: CONCATENATE joins cell values with separator and skips empty cells
behaviors.justification: Real failures: wrong separator used, empty cells treated as blank strings (not skipped), type coercion (numbers not converted to strings), null handling. Requires actual cell grid with mixed content (values, empty cells, different types) to trigger these edge cases.
behaviors.test_description: Create cells: A1="Hello", A2="World", A3=(empty), A4=123. Create B1=CONCATENATE(A1, "-", A2, "-", A3, "-", A4). Verify result is "Hello-World-123" (A3 skipped, 123 converted). Test with all empty cells, all filled, and verify separator placement.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing a cell in a different sheet (e.g., =Sheet2!A1+1) resolves correctly when the sheet exists
behaviors.justification: Cross-sheet references require multiple sheets and a sheet name resolver. Real failures: sheet not found, wrong sheet accessed, cell within sheet not found. Cannot be caught without multi-sheet workbook structure.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with Sheet1 and Sheet2. In Sheet2.A1, put value 5. In Sheet1.B1, create =Sheet2!A1+1. Verify B1 evaluates to 6. Test with non-existent sheet (Sheet3) and verify an error is raised. Test referencing different cells across sheets.

behaviors.description: When a referenced cell's value changes, all dependent formulas are recalculated in correct topological order
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the recalculation engine's topological sort and propagation logic. Real failures: formulas recalculated in wrong order (C1 updated before B1), some dependents missed, circular dependencies cause infinite recursion. Requires multiple formulas with dependencies and actual value mutation.
behaviors.test_description: Build a dependency tree: A1=1, B1=A1*2, C1=B1+10, D1=B1+C1. Evaluate all cells. Change A1 to 2. Verify that B1, C1, D1 are all recalculated (no stale values) in topological order. Assert final values: B1=4, C1=14, D1=18. Test with deep chains to catch order bugs.

behaviors.description: Undo after formula edit restores both formula text and all previously computed dependent values
behaviors.justification: Real failures: undo doesn't restore formula text (only values), dependent values not restored to pre-edit state, undo/redo stack not properly managed. The failure boundary spans formula editing, dependency tracking, caching/memoization, and undo/redo systems. All must work together.
behaviors.test_description: Create A1=5, B1=A1*2 (value 10), C1=B1+1 (value 11). Edit B1 to A1*3. Verify B1 now equals 15 and C1 equals 16. Undo the edit. Verify B1 formula is restored to =A1*2, B1 value is 10, and C1 value is 11. Test multi-step undo (multiple edits, undo all).

behaviors.description: Saving workbook to disk and loading it back produces identical cell values and formulas with no data loss or precision change
behaviors.justification: Real failures: binary format loses metadata, floating-point precision lost during serialization (endianness, format choice), formula text corrupted during round-trip, cell references become invalid. The failure boundary includes serialization logic, file I/O, deserialization, and floating-point handling. Requires actual disk writes/reads.
behaviors.test_description: Create a workbook with: A1=3.14159265359, B1="text", C1=, D1=SUM(A1:B1). Save to disk. Close and reload the file. Verify A1 has same precision, B1="text", C1 is empty, D1 formula is unchanged and evaluates correctly. Test with large worksheets, special characters in formulas, and various data types.
